speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number two. You were talking about your cousins being taken away to Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I said, we cried for them. But later, we were thinking, why didn't we jump on that truck and being taken? We could have-- there were many-- the roads, the highways were bombed. But when they reached Siberia-- which is a cold, desolate land-- they were prisoners working. There was-- some had frozen toes from the cold. There was typhoid epidemic. But the chances for survival were greater. Some survived and came home and found emptiness and their parents killed. Their home was gone. But we felt we should have ran. But when you have your roots down, when you have a family, have your home, it's hard to uproot yourself and run to the unknown, especially when we did not anticipate such tragedy. The Russians, br-- their families occupied nice homes previously owned by people-- by Jewish people-- who were middle-class people. They were-- they had propaganda films. You go to the movies, and then two hours of endless propaganda about Stalin and Lenin.

speaker: interviewer
text: They indoctrinate our children in school, saying, God-- the children hold their hand. God, give us candy. Please give us candy. Nothing happened. And then they said, Father, [NON-ENGLISH] Stalin, Father Stalin, please give us candy. And the door opened, and they came in and handed candy to the children. You say, see? God doesn't give you anything, but fa-- Stalin gives you. So people were unhappy about that situation. People were hesitant, going to the synagogues, to the churches. They didn't want to-- they did not want to tell-- show that they are religious. It was a fearful time.

speaker: interviewee
text: We would go to a movie theater, and somebody would grab you by the collar and say, get out! And give the seat to their wives because they liberated us. I would not say that the government policy is such, but there were some rude people. They were primitive. If they went to a movie, like military movie, where they have military go-- propaganda, there were only several men's rooms. So they will go out to a side street and, in broad light, relieve themself. The next day, from the sanitation, had to clean up. The women were dressed shabbily because they were poor. The bought out everything they could see and shipped to Russia. They did not give-- bring-- import anything-- only watermelons. But when I think of the Nazis and the Germans, that complaint is very minimal. So-- but I'm just relating our impression at that time. In 1941, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, the G-- the Germans violated their pact with Russia and attacked. And there was confusion in our town, because the first thing, the Russians had to flee with their families. They were packing up and running. Naturally, the Communists were the first one to run, because if the Germans come, they would be the first ones to be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were some single people who, who ran with Russia-- not that they were Communists. Again, why didn't we run with the Russians? We didn't like them. And we had our home, and we could not-- we did not anticipate what's going to-- that such tragedy's going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, because our highway not far from us, the main road, was filled with tanks, the Russians, they were posing resistance but retreating to fortify themselves on their territory using our territory as a buffer. And bombs were flying-- falling, and artillery, and anti-craft artillery. And we were, again, in the cellar with our tenants, neighbors, and for several days. And my father looked out from the window, from our cellar, and where the road from the courtyard to where our tool shacks were, he saw flames, fire. And it just happened that at that moment, the planes that were flying and bombing subsided. He ran out, and he saw our house, under the roof, coming out, flames, tongues of flames coming out. He screamed, my house is on fire! And he ran upstairs. And upstairs, there was a ladder on the side over here, put up the ladder. He went up under the roof in the attic. We had pails of sand that was like preparation for defense. If there's a fire, you put it out. But the Germans threw incendiary bombs that come like in bunches. It's not like a fi-- normal fire. It's an explosion, and that spreads very quickly. The attic was full of smoke and flames. And he couldn't breathe, and he had to retreat. He came down. He ran into the apartment and started throwing out, through the-- from the window, things, whatever he can grab-- bedding, things, whatever. And in meantime, we were outside. We ran outside from the cellar because of the house was on fire. And mach-- airplanes fly, machine-gun-- gunning on the running people, on us. And bombs are falling. And smoke, and we cannot see each other. And we are screaming, daddy, come out! We were afraid-- our veranda was wood, wooden, so that he'll be trapped. We were screaming. But he, without stopping, was throwing out whatever, save things from the fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother, in one moment, took up courage and she ran upstairs. While she was upstairs in the kitchen, there was an explosion. She grabbed the teakettle and ran out. She didn't say-- she said, why didn't I take the silver, something else?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father finally-- the fire was-- flame was already in the windows, coming down. So my father ran out. We took sheets of [INAUDIBLE] and made bundles and dragged it. Now we are on the street. Across, our Ukranian neighbors, their house is on fire. The whole street, whichever way we turn, houses were on fire. The cross-- and across the street, this [INAUDIBLE] that goes this way was on fire. We are so-- we are so like in the middle, no place to run. Not far from us, there was a warehouse of spirits, and that caused explosions. We, we thought we're going to be burned to death. And then my brother [? Munio ?], Max-- I keep-- forgot that we had a puppy that we-- that in fear, and while he was running, he dropped. He forgot. He ran into the flaming cellar and saved it. And the street, the, the movie theater near us was on fire all around. There's no place to run to. Somebody yelled-- in the corner, there was a church, a Ukrainian church. The church is not on fire. And on [? Lichnyovska ?] Street, there was another Ukrainian church, was also not bombed, which proved that the Germans had the blueprint. They know where everything is. And this was a Jewish-- predominantly Jewish-- neighborhood. They bombed, but they spared the Ukrainian churches. They flew very low, throwing those incendiary bombs. And on the main highway, they threw heavy bombs to bomb the tanks, the retreating Russians. We were hysterical. And in the-- in the smoke and fire, we couldn't see each other. My father's eyes were bloodshot. He was all with soots. And we are running towards the church. We can hardly see. My sister, Laura, and I ran into the church. And as we went into the church, the statue of Jesus Christ by the altar was standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in that hysterical-- state of hysteria, we threw ourselves in front of that statue, screaming, Jesus, Jesus, help us, help us, crying because we would have prayed to Allah, even though Jewish people believe only in the invisible God. But driven by fear, we turned to Jesus Christ for help. Then we dragged our bundles away from the altar on the side. And we laid there. Can I ret-- retreat to another, which I forgot to mention? Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, our children obtained a puppy. And we children were thinking, what should we name that puppy? We heard of names-- Nero, Caesar, another tyrant of history. And we knew Hitler's our enemy. He's a dog. So children said, we'll call him Hitler, then like it's, it's an insult to Hitler. We called him, Hitler, Hitler!

speaker: interviewer
text: But we realized that the puppy was very affectionate, licking our faces. kiss-- we were kissing him on the mouth. And my mother said, don't kiss the dog on the mouth. He licks everything in the street. But we didn't listen. We loved him. And we realized, how could we call a pet that we loved Hitler? We can't-- we cannot kiss him and call him Hitler. So we changed it to Rex. Then it was too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a Ukrainian population who-- Polish citizens, but they were enemies to Poland because that territory, Eastern Poland, was Ukraine once. And for the Poles conquered it. So they prayed and hoped that someday, they'll retrieve that land. It was the breadbasket of Poland, very rich land.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they hoped that the Germans will occupy Poland, if they collaborate with the Germans, when the Germans come to Poland, in reciprocation, they'll give them that part, though they had underground activities and [? dreamed ?]. We did not know-- we did not realize that calling that puppy Hitler agitated our Ukrainians. That was a blunder. Even though we changed the name on our own, that we loved our puppy, it was too late. They couldn't help saying, why wouldn't Abraham or Moses not be more appropriate?

speaker: interviewee
text: We w-- we knew that, God forbid, if the German would come, they would point at us out. At one time, we went visiting our relatives. And upon our return, we opened the gate, and at the foot of the steps laid our dog, outstretched, dead, poisoned. The children, we were hysterical-- crying, frightened. But more frightened were my parents. That was symbolic. That dog had to die, even though they heard we called him Rex, because that was an insult to their fuhrer, to Hitler. And we had a foreboding of danger. But time elapsed, and we had the Russians. The Russians came, which I'm sure the Ukrainians anticipated the Germans to march in. And now the Russ-- the Germans are about to enter. We are being-- we are in church, in a Ukrainian church, not feeling so comfortable, but they let us be there. And it was more insulting that our dog would crawl away and crawl near the statues, which is improper. We laid there on blankets at the church, and bombs were falling. And the church is high, so the impact is stronger. And glass is falling down, and we are huddling. That if the church is hit, we're finished because it's [? hell. ?] My father was exhausted from saving the stuff. And he kept saying, I couldn't save my house. I couldn't save my house. And when there was a period when the planes retreated momentarily, he walked to the gate of the church and stood there, and saw our house collapse. The roofs cave in and tongues of flame coming out of the windows. And he wept, and he cried, smudge with his soots, broken up.

speaker: interviewer
text: My brother, [? Munio, ?] went over to him, embraced him. He said, daddy, don't cry for the house. If we will survive this war, and we'll be all together, a family, nobody will be missing, we'll consider ourselves very lucky people. And my father said, but what will you do when nightfall comes and you'll have no place to put your head down, no home to go into? And I was thinking my brother had that wisdom of-- that he knew what's com-- he felt that-- he felt that danger is coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainians, their homes, our neighbors', was burned down, too. But their heart was light. They anticipated that their benefactor will come. The Germans will march, and they'll have their land back. And during quiet moments, they sang songs. We had a foreboding of tragedy and trouble. But they were civil with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were able to-- we-- for three days, we were laying down blankets. And whenever we heard pla-- airplanes coming and the whistle of the bombs coming, we shriveled up. My sister and I were lying down on a blanket, and I felt, what do I have, a rock in my pocket? What is it? Those were our hip bones. From the anxiety, from the fear, in a few days, we lost weight from not-- not from not eating-- from the fear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few days, the Russians were retreating. The bombing stopped, and the Germans were marching in. And we hear-- the earth was shaking because the highway was not far. And as the Germans went forward, the Germans occu-- began to occupy Brody. For the Germ-- for the Ukrainian, it was time to have a celebration in church. So we had to vacant, vacant out the church and go out. And they are cleaning up the church. Our neighbors who did not have their homes, they had other Ukrainians who came and invited them to their homes. And they knew it's going to even be better because they'll-- they're victims. Maybe they'll give them the most beautiful home of the Jews, even though their home was nice. We came out with our bundles and looked at the ruins of our house, still smoldering, still that stench of smoke. Our beautiful garden-- leaves were shriveled up, the ones that were closer to the house. Further down, deeper in the garden, it didn't burn down. Still traces of our-- the fence burned down. That little house in the garden, underneath it, we had also a cement cellar where we used to storage the food on the shelves. And it was a cooler. You walked down-- you-- in that little house, you lifted the floor, a door. It went down to a cemented cellar, where we-- all our foods, and butter and milk-- we had no refrigeration in those years. Even our tenants were allowed to do it. Only the steps were there, the cement steps. The whole-- that beautiful little, like gingerbread house, was gone. And with it, the materials that my father saved, which we had for the custom-making suits, was burned out. Everything was gone which we could have had to survive later, for trading in for food. My father looked around, looked in the ruins. And then he went-- there was one side of the house, which was to the rear, and he looks. The upper level of the house collapsed. And because when my father supervised the house, he knew everything. There were steel beams, so when it collapsed, that part of a cellar remained, didn't go through. It did not go through. And so my father goes around the bend, around the house, and he sees the wooden doors are there. He pries them open. There are steps. And that part of a cellar is there. It's hot. It's impossible to go in, but it's there. And my father said, we may be able to live in it. Being that everything collapsed, we have a roof. The metal from the roof caved in. Everything caved in on top. But it was hot. We couldn't go in there. So we slept for two nights in the garden, spread under the trees. My father's sister, Pearl, and Rachel-- Rojo-- came-- when it was quiet down-- came running, seeing if we were-- we are alive. When they saw our house burned down, they cried. But then they said, you are alive. And they said, look. They had apartments and children. We'll take Rosa. We'll take Laura. A few children, they take to their house. And the other sister will take us where we would stay. And my father said, we have a boding-- we have a foreboding of what's coming. We want to be toge-- together. We don't want to separate. And the idea that the cellar is there gave him that we're going to be together. We're not going to go. But my aunts went back and brought us food. We were sitting in the garden. And then there were-- people were starting to go into the church, toward that side street-- processions and singing and praying. To them, it was a holiday. In meantime, my father, knowing that we have a place to, to sleep and to stay gave him courage. So they went. They said, we'll need some furniture, some beds, something, when we move into that cellar when it cools off. There were many bombed-out houses and burned down, so that there was raw material, wood. So he gathered wooden boards, dragged them, and they're going to make makeshift beds, makeshift table, like bunk beds. We didn't have nails, so with a rock, we found nails in some ruins. And then when my Aunt Rachel came, she went back to her house and got us a hammer and got nails, so he-- he was able to do it. And he started-- my brothers got busy. And when you are busy physically, it's helpful when you're broken up. So my father sort of gained back that energy, knowing that we have a place where to put-- lay our heads down. He made those beds, like bunk beds. They were not perfect, but they were-- now-- and boards across. And then they went to the outskirts of town to a farm and got straw, bunches of straw. And they got some prod-- products there. And we were waiting when it's cooled off to move in. And we moved into our cellar after three days sleeping in a garden. We moved in with those bunk beds and like our belongings, whatever he saved. The bedding was there. My aunts brought us some extra pots and pans and things. And my father said, thank you, God, that I'm still on my property.

speaker: interviewer
text: Exhausted from all that and from the anticipation of what's coming, we went to sleep. We fell asleep. In the middle of the night, we heard loud explosions. And we thought, the Germans are here. Why is there still shooting? It was thunder and lightning, and a storm, and the rain. But we felt we were safe. We had a petroleum lamp that we could use.

speaker: interviewee
text: Suddenly-- when the roof caved in, it caved in like in a cone shape. So when the water accumulated, it came down like a funnel. And we started feeling water coming down. With the lightning, we could see water coming down. So where there was a barrel, my father put it under, and it started pouring in. But then, in another corner, a piece of plaster fell down, and water came. And we h-- were hugging against the wall. And because we-- my father made a bunk bed upper, we were underneath, huddling together. And the water kept coming. No more-- we could not stop it with any pots, with anything. It was just pouring down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And lightening and thunder, and we're afraid. Are we going to drown? Or the ceiling is going to be wet and collapse on us. But because it was a bunk bed, we thought maybe that wood would hold it back. The puppy tried to crawl under, but when the water started coming in, we picked him up and we, we took-- we held onto him. He was shivering. And we cried. My parents cried, because they cried for us. We thought that we were going to die because the ceiling is going to collapse on us. There's no place to run to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even today, whenever there's thunder and rain, I never-- it always, I relive that moment and that fear. But this time, I feel we outsmarted the elements. They cannot reach us. I'm safe. It's something you don't forget. The-- we're not-- we're not afraid of the Germans. We're afraid a ceiling will collapse and kill us. We're going to drown. There's no place to run to. And the rain stopped. Because it was with such an impact, it stopped. But we-- it's still darkness. We sat there, shivering, cold. Next morning, the sun was shining. My brothers and my father got busy dragging out the bedding, which was soaked, dragging out whatever, clothing, and cleaning the water, draining out of the water with pails and then with rags, whatever, with towels, emptying out. And it was damp, and we were sitting outside. We were so worn out, we didn't care anymore. And we spread the bedding and everything. We cannot go in. Everything is wet. And then we heard-- there-- the, the Germans, when they came in, they issued an order that they need a Jewish committee-- they're called the Judenrat-- a committee that whatever orders they give to the Jews-- whatever orders they give to the Jews would have to come through a committee. They're not going to come to individual Jews, making demands. Somebody came, knocked at our-- and told my father, if he wants to join that committee. And my father was broken up. He said, I, I lost my home. I am wiped out. Perhaps people whose home was not destroyed will have more, who are better in a position to be active now. I'm physically destroyed. He can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they picked the eldest and prominent people of the town, that they should be in charge, that they could represent the Jewish community. We did not know anything because we were sitting on our premises. But there were activities going on in town already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we waited for the bedding to dry up. We should be able to go. And the sun was out, and after the whole day turning, we were able to go in, move back. It was damp, but we had to have a shelter. And then we knew that we have no roof, that when it rains, it comes down to us. So my father got busy. We had to get new straw because the straw was wet. He went-- they went to the farms and got more, fresh bundles of straw. And the sun was hot, so the sheets and everything was dry. And some things were left still outside. Just the necessity-- necessities, we should be able to move in again. Then my father said, it's important to fix the roof. They climbed up on the roof and started removing the metal sheets and put it on a pitch, which created like an attic. They-- with poles, he supported. And the, the, the fl-- which was the floor, this our ceiling. Those steel beams were strong, so he could walk on it. It was safe. Besides, it was with rubble. So my father made-- covered the cellar like in a pitch, like a tent, and supported it with beams, and put all those metal sheets on it. So if the water comes, it's going to roll off. It's not going to come to us. They got busy, and we moved into the cellar. And then the Germans issued orders-- a curfew at 4:00. There's not to be-- no Jew is to be in the street because they'll be shot. In the summ-- in the summer, it's-- the sun is shining, daylight. We cannot dare-- we don't dare to stick our heads out. Besides, with the house that was burned down, there was like a skeleton of a house. The windows burned out. It was just outlines of windows. And where we were, we didn't want anybody. And the whole neighborhood was burned out. It was like a shelter. We were-- felt safe, that nobody knows that behind, in the back of that house, there's an entrance to a cellar. There was a window to the street, so my father cut out that window and filled it up with bricks, that the front street should not have been visible that somebody is there. So it was just like a ruin. Nobody would know that we are there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to-- we were not allowed to be in the street. And now if we had to go in the street, they issued that every Jew has to wear an armband with a star of David. So from sheets, we cut out strips, and we were sewing and embroidering like a little star of David.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were afraid to walk the streets. It was full of military, and they occupied. The Gestapo was there occupying quarters. And after a few days, things normalized, which means whoever had a home that did not burn down had a home. We stayed in our ruin.
